does: This paper introduces stage-wise model diffing, a method for identifying and analyzing differences between neural network models through dictionary fine-tuning. The approach systematically extracts interpretable features and representations at different stages of model training or across different model checkpoints, then uses fine-tuned dictionaries to characterize how models diverge in their learned representations. The method enables structured comparison of what distinct models have learned at various layers and training phases, providing a framework to visualize and quantify the differences in feature spaces and activation patterns between models.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in mechanistic interpretability and feature visualization, particularly sparse autoencoders and dictionary learning approaches that decompose neural network activations into human-interpretable components. It extends prior research on model comparison and analysis that typically relied on shallow metrics like weight differences or final performance gaps. The paper connects to interpretability literature investigating how models learn different internal representations, and to work on understanding neural network training dynamics across checkpoints. It leverages dictionary fine-tuning techniques developed for activation analysis to enable systematic cross-model comparison rather than single-model interpretation.
enables: This framework opens directions for understanding when and why different training procedures or architectures lead to divergent learned representations. It could facilitate debugging model behavior by pinpointing exactly where models diverge in feature learning, support research on model consolidation and merging by identifying compatible vs. incompatible representations, and enable new analyses of generalization and robustness by comparing how different models solve the same task. The stage-wise approach particularly enables longitudinal analysis of how models' internal structure changes during training, potentially revealing principles of representation learning and offering tools for quality control and model selection in practice.
